{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|[[wiktionary:産土神|産土神]], lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019年4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>

In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019年4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|[[wiktionary:産土神|産土神]], lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019年4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|[[wiktionary:産土神|産土神]], lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>

While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|[[wiktionary:産土神|産土神]], lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]
== References == <references />
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|last=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}
]]
]]
]]

While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />

== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|[[wiktionary:産土神|産土神]], lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>

== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|[[wiktionary:産土神|産土神]], lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|[[wiktionary:産土神|産土神]], lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]
== References == <references />
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|last=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}]] {{Shinto shrine}}

"Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />

Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|[[wiktionary:産土神|産土神]], lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]

In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: mark page as missing translated-page template
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[city]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]
== References == <references />
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|last=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}]] {{Shinto shrine}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Tutelary deities]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Shinto terminology]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:Regional deities]]
[[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[sn:Ubusunagami]]}}

Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
