[[wiktionary:産土神|産土神]] [[sn:Ubusunagami]] [[en:Ubusunagami]] [[sn:busunagami]] [[sn: failed]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:産土神]] [[ko:산토신]] [[zh:產土神]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove obsolete category Pages with valid en interwikis
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]
== References == <references />
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|last=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}]] {{Shinto shrine}}

Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[:en:Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]

== Bibliography ==

== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />

Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. [[wiktionary:産土神|産土神]] [[sn:Ubusunagami]] [[en:Ubusunagami]] [[sn:busunagami]] [[sn: failed]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:産土神]] [[ko:산토신]] [[zh:產土神]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1392097]] Wikidata name: ubusunagami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[:en:Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1392097]] Wikidata name: ubusunagami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[:en:Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>

While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ubusunagami]][[ja:産土神]][[ko:산토신]][[zh:產土神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1392097]] Wikidata name: ubusunagami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[:en:Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>

Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

[[en:Ubusunagami]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:産土神]] [[ko:산토신]] [[zh:產土神]] {{wikidata link|Q1392097}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ubusunagami]][[ja:産土神]][[ko:산토신]][[zh:產土神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1392097]] Wikidata name: ubusunagami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[:en:Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]

[[wiktionary:産土神|産土神]]
[[en:Ubusunagami]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:産土神]] [[ko:산토신]] [[zh:產土神]] {{wikidata link|Q1392097}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ubusunagami]][[ja:産土神]][[ko:산토신]][[zh:產土神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1392097]] Wikidata name: ubusunagami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[:en:Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ubusunagami]][[ja:産土神]][[ko:산토신]][[zh:產土神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1392097]] Wikidata name: ubusunagami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[:en:Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]
== References == <references />
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|last=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}]] <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ubusunagami]][[ja:産土神]][[ko:산토신]][[zh:產土神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1392097]] Wikidata name: ubusunagami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''''Ubusunagami'''''|, lit. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[:en:Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]

"Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[:en:Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]

"Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[:en:Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>

"Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary]] [[kami]] of one's [[:en:Place of birth|birthplace]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a [[:en:City|City]] that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in [[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]
== References == <references />
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|last=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}]] <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->
[[wiktionary:産土神|産土神]]

For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. "Birth Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} in Shinto are {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=tutelary}} [[kami]] of one's {{ill|Place of birth|en|Place of birth|WD=Q1322263|lt=birthplace}}.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9708 |access-date=2022-02-20 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == In Shinto, a guardian deity of the land of one's birth.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|author=日本史用語研究会|title=必携日本史用語|publisher=実教出版|isbn=9784407316599|edition=四訂版|series=|date=2 February 2009 |author-link=|access-date=|orig-date=2009-2-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 第２版,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=産土神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%94%A3%E5%9C%9F%E7%A5%9E-35082|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is believed to be a deity that protects you from before you are born until after you die, and that it will protect you throughout your life even if you move to another place.<ref name="yougo" />
While the relationship between [[Ujigami]] and [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] is based on blood relations, that between Ubusunagami and Ubuko is based on a sense of faith based on geographical relations.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> Therefore, it is in a {{ill|City|en|City|WD=Q515|lt=City}} that this consciousness is strongly expressed. For example, in Kyoto, the unity of clan groups weakened, and a sense of community based on geographical ties was formed, and in the Middle Ages, powerful {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s such as [[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Inari Shrine]], Goryo Shrine, [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Shrine]] were established. Here the concept of '''birthplace area''' was developed based on the god of birthplace. The term ''birthplace pilgrimage'' came into general use, and the custom of paying a visit to the birthplace for a child's [[Miyamairi]],<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" /> coming-of-age ceremony, [[Shichi-Go-San]], etc. became popular. In addition, in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}}, Hiezanou was regarded as the birthplace deity of the [[Tokugawa clan]], and its rituals were extremely grand.
Ubusunagami are distinct from Chinjugami because one maintains the link to their Ubusunagami throughout their entire life, even if they move to a new location.
Nonetheless Ubusunagami, [[Ujigami]], and [[Chinjugami]] are often conflated in the modern day,<ref>{{Cite web|year=2004|editor3-link=2019th year 4月24日|title=神社とまつりの知識 氏神・産土神・鎮守神|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090220064030/http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|archive-date=2009-02-20|access-date=2009-03-26|publisher=[[Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=鎮守神|url=https://dic.pixiv.net/a/%E9%8E%AE%E5%AE%88%E7%A5%9E|access-date=2022-02-13|website=ピクシブ百科事典|language=ja}}{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}</ref>{{User-generated source|date=January 2024}}<ref name="osaki">{{cite web|title=Jinja to Matsuri no Chishiki|url=http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/knowlege/deities.html|access-date=20 July 2011|website=Ujigami, ubusunagami, chinjusha|publisher=Hachiman-gū|language=ja}}</ref> all three are seen as strengthening local identity.<ref>{{Cite web|last=小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|title=守護神とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AE%88%E8%AD%B7%E7%A5%9E-77679|access-date=2022-02-20|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
In some locations, the ubusunagami is linked to the ubugami, a tutelary deity of infants and pregnant women; in these cases, it is customary to pay respects immediately following childbirth at a {{ill|Hokora|en|Hokora|WD=Q1442984|lt=Hokora}} to the deity.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2022a" />
Since the Muromachi period, the belief in the Ujigami as a family deity is declining nationwide and is being absorbed by the newly emerged belief in the Ubusunagami and Chinjugami.<ref name="shinto-history2">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|title=Shinto, a short history|last2=Breen|first2=John|last3=Inoue|first3=Nobutaka|last4=Itō|first4=Satoshi|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2003|isbn=0-415-31179-9}}</ref>
== In popular culture == Ubusunagami are prominent in {{ill|Jujutsu Kaisen|en|Jujutsu Kaisen|WD=Q55522899|lt=Jujutsu Kaisen}}.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Hitchcock |last2=Kubrick |last3=Medium |first3=while his favorite film writer is Roger Ebert Twitter |date=2023-08-04 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What Are the Ubusunagami & Why Are They Important? |url=https://fictionhorizon.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-the-ubusunagami-why-are-they-important/ |access-date=2023-12-07 |language=en-US}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Ujigami]]
== References == <references />
== Bibliography ==
